articles.headline: Mahatma Gandhi's 155th Birth Anniversary: Reaffirming Principles of Truth, Non-Violence, and Sarvodaya in Modern India
articles.explainer:
• India commemorates the 155th birth anniversary of Mahatma Gandhi, the Father of the Nation, with various events across the country.
• The day is globally observed as the International Day of Non-Violence, emphasizing Gandhi's philosophy of peace and satyagraha.
• Discussions focus on the contemporary relevance of Gandhian ideals in addressing global challenges like conflict, inequality, and environmental degradation.
• Government initiatives like Swachh Bharat Abhiyan and promotion of Khadi are often linked to Gandhi's vision of rural upliftment and self-reliance.
• Leaders pay homage at Raj Ghat, highlighting Gandhi's enduring legacy in India's freedom struggle and nation-building.
gsSubTopics: Modern Indian history from about the middle of the eighteenth century until the present- significant events, personalities, issues
The Freedom Struggle - its various stages and important contributors/contributions from different parts of the country
prelimsPoints: Mahatma Gandhi's birth date: October 2, 1869, Porbandar, Gujarat.
International Day of Non-Violence declared by UN General Assembly in 2007.
Key Gandhian principles: Truth (Satya), Non-violence (Ahimsa), Satyagraha, Sarvodaya (welfare of all), Swadeshi.
Important movements led by Gandhi: Champaran, Kheda, Non-Cooperation, Civil Disobedience, Quit India.
Raj Ghat in Delhi is the memorial to Mahatma Gandhi.
mainsPoints: Discuss the enduring relevance of Gandhian philosophy in contemporary India's socio-economic and political landscape.
Analyze how Gandhi's principles of non-violence and truth can be applied to resolve modern-day conflicts and promote global peace.
Evaluate the success and challenges of government initiatives (e.g., Swachh Bharat, Khadi promotion) in embodying Gandhian ideals.
Examine Gandhi's concept of 'Sarvodaya' and its implications for inclusive development and social justice.
Critically assess the role of Gandhi's philosophy in shaping India's foreign policy, particularly its emphasis on peace and multilateralism.
articles.imageDescription: Mahatma Gandhi's portrait with a spinning wheel, symbolizing his ideals of self-reliance and peace.
articles.trivia: Mahatma Gandhi was nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize five times between 1937 and 1948 but never received it.

articles.headline: Swachh Bharat Mission: A Decade of Progress and the Road Ahead for Sustainable Sanitation
articles.explainer:
• Launched on Gandhi Jayanti in 2014, the Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM) completes a decade, marking significant strides in sanitation coverage.
• The mission achieved Open Defecation Free (ODF) status in rural areas, leading to improved public health indicators.
• Phase II of SBM (SBM-Grameen Phase II and SBM-Urban 2.0) focuses on sustaining ODF status and holistic solid and liquid waste management.
• Challenges remain in behavioral change, infrastructure maintenance, and transitioning towards a circular economy for waste.
• Government emphasizes citizen participation (Jan Andolan) and technological solutions for effective waste management.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: SBM launched on Oct 2, 2014.
Two sub-missions: SBM-Urban (Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs) and SBM-Grameen (Ministry of Jal Shakti).
SBM Phase I focused on achieving ODF status.
SBM Phase II (2020-21 to 2024-25) focuses on ODF Plus, ODF Plus Plus, and SLWM.
ODF Plus aims for visual cleanliness and ODF Plus Plus for safe containment and treatment of fecal sludge.
mainsPoints: Analyze the impact of Swachh Bharat Mission on public health, gender equality, and environmental sustainability in India.
Discuss the challenges in achieving and sustaining 'ODF Plus' status across all regions, particularly in urban areas.
Evaluate the role of behavioral change communication and community participation in the success of SBM.
Suggest innovative approaches for solid and liquid waste management to transition towards a circular economy model.
Examine how SBM aligns with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly SDG 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation).
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting the evolution of Swachh Bharat Mission from ODF to ODF Plus.
articles.trivia: Swachh Bharat Abhiyan is the largest sanitation initiative ever undertaken by any country.

articles.headline: Khadi and Village Industries Commission (KVIC) Intensifies Efforts for Rural Employment and Sustainable Livelihoods
articles.explainer:
• On Gandhi Jayanti, KVIC reiterates its commitment to promoting Khadi and village industries, aligning with Gandhi's vision of 'Gram Swaraj'.
• Initiatives focus on skill development, technological upgrades, and market linkages for artisans in rural areas.
• Khadi sales have seen a significant boost in recent years, contributing to the economic empowerment of weavers and rural communities.
• The sector plays a crucial role in job creation, particularly for women and marginalized sections.
• Government policies aim to integrate Khadi products into mainstream markets and promote them globally.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it
articles.source: Ministry of Micro, Small & Medium Enterprises
prelimsPoints: KVIC is a statutory body formed under the Khadi and Village Industries Commission Act, 1956.
It functions under the Ministry of Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSME).
Mandate is to plan, promote, facilitate, organize and assist in the establishment and development of Khadi and village industries.
Prime Minister's Employment Generation Programme (PMEGP) is a credit-linked subsidy scheme implemented by KVIC.
Khadi is a hand-spun and hand-woven natural fiber cloth.
mainsPoints: Analyze the role of Khadi and Village Industries in fostering inclusive growth and sustainable development in rural India.
Discuss the challenges faced by the Khadi sector in terms of modernization, market competition, and attracting younger generations.
Evaluate government policies and schemes aimed at promoting Khadi and village industries for employment generation and poverty alleviation.
Examine how the 'Gram Swaraj' concept can be revitalized through contemporary approaches to rural industrialization.
Suggest strategies for enhancing the global appeal and market penetration of Khadi products.
articles.imageDescription: Artisans working on traditional Charkha (spinning wheel) with Khadi fabric.
articles.trivia: The 'Charkha' (spinning wheel) was adopted as a symbol of the Indian independence movement by Mahatma Gandhi.

articles.headline: Global Call for Peace: UN Observes International Day of Non-Violence on Gandhi Jayanti
articles.explainer:
• The United Nations marks October 2nd as the International Day of Non-Violence, reinforcing Mahatma Gandhi's message of peace.
• The day serves as a reminder to resolve conflicts through peaceful means, dialogue, and mutual understanding.
• UN Secretary-General issues a statement, urging nations to uphold principles of non-violence in an increasingly complex global landscape.
• India plays a pivotal role in advocating for non-violence in international forums, drawing from its Gandhian heritage.
• Educational institutions and civil society organizations worldwide organize events to promote non-violence and tolerance.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: UN General Assembly Resolution A/RES/61/271 adopted on 15 June 2007, establishing October 2 as International Day of Non-Violence.
The resolution reaffirms 'the universal relevance of the principle of non-violence'.
The day aims to 'disseminate the message of non-violence, including through education and public awareness'.
Mahatma Gandhi's philosophy of non-violence is central to the observance.
UN promotes the culture of peace through various initiatives.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the International Day of Non-Violence in promoting global peace and security in the 21st century.
Analyze India's contribution to global peace initiatives and its diplomatic efforts rooted in Gandhian principles.
Examine the challenges to non-violence in a world grappling with terrorism, geopolitical tensions, and social unrest.
Evaluate the role of international organizations like the UN in fostering a culture of peace and tolerance.
How can education and public awareness campaigns effectively propagate the message of non-violence among youth globally?
articles.imageDescription: UN logo alongside a dove of peace, symbolizing international cooperation for non-violence.
articles.trivia: The phrase 'Ahimsa Paramo Dharma' (non-violence is the highest moral virtue) is a central tenet of Jainism, influencing Gandhi.

articles.headline: Women's Reservation Act, 2023: Delimitation Process and Implementation Timeline in Focus for 2024 Elections
articles.explainer:
• Following the passage of the Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam (Women's Reservation Act) in 2023, discussions intensify on its implementation.
• The Act mandates 33% reservation for women in Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies, but requires a fresh census and delimitation.
• Political parties and civil society groups debate the timeline for delimitation and its potential impact on electoral constituencies.
• Concerns are raised regarding the delay in implementation and the need for clarity on rotational reservation.
• The Election Commission of India and the Delimitation Commission are expected to play crucial roles in this process.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
prelimsPoints: The 128th Constitutional Amendment Bill, 2023, became the Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam (106th Constitutional Amendment Act).
It reserves one-third of seats for women in Lok Sabha, State Assemblies, and Delhi Assembly.
The reservation will come into effect after the next census and subsequent delimitation exercise.
The reservation will be for a period of 15 years and can be extended by Parliament.
Seats will be reserved on a rotational basis after each delimitation exercise.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the significance and potential impact of the Women's Reservation Act on gender equality and political representation in India.
Discuss the constitutional and logistical challenges involved in the delimitation process and its implications for the Act's implementation.
Evaluate the arguments for and against the necessity of a fresh census and delimitation for the Act's operationalization.
Examine how the rotational reservation of seats for women might affect political stability and constituency development.
Suggest measures to ensure the effective and timely implementation of the Women's Reservation Act to empower women in governance.
articles.imageDescription: Symbolic image of women participating in parliamentary proceedings.
articles.trivia: The concept of women's reservation was first introduced in India in local self-governing bodies through the 73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendment Acts.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Global Headwinds, Focus on Inflation Management
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concludes its bi-monthly review, opting to keep the repo rate unchanged.
• The decision reflects a cautious approach to balance inflation control with supporting economic growth amidst global uncertainties.
• RBI highlights concerns over volatile food prices and geopolitical tensions impacting the inflation trajectory.
• GDP growth projections are maintained, but risks from uneven monsoon and global slowdown are acknowledged.
• The central bank reaffirms its commitment to anchoring inflation expectations and ensuring financial stability.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary policy
prelimsPoints: Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) consists of 6 members (3 from RBI, 3 appointed by Government).
Repo rate is the rate at which RBI lends money to commercial banks.
RBI's primary objective is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Inflation target for CPI: 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
Liquidity Adjustment Facility (LAF) consists of repo and reverse repo operations.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate status quo and its implications for India's economic growth and inflation.
Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in managing inflation, particularly due to supply-side shocks and global factors.
Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in stimulating economic growth while ensuring financial stability.
Examine the interplay between fiscal policy and monetary policy in achieving macroeconomic objectives in India.
Critically assess the impact of global economic headwinds and geopolitical tensions on India's monetary policy decisions and economic outlook.
articles.imageDescription: RBI headquarters building in Mumbai, symbolizing financial authority.
articles.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

articles.headline: India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) Model Gains Global Traction, Facilitating Inclusive Growth
articles.explainer:
• India's pioneering Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) stack, including Aadhaar, UPI, and DigiLocker, is being recognized globally for its transformative potential.
• Several developing nations are exploring adopting India's DPI model to enhance financial inclusion, governance, and service delivery.
• The G20 presidency in 2023 highlighted India's DPI as a model for sustainable development goals.
• Discussions focus on data privacy, cybersecurity, and interoperability challenges in scaling DPI globally.
• India advocates for open-source, inclusive, and interoperable digital solutions to bridge the global digital divide.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Cyber security
prelimsPoints: India's DPI comprises three layers: Identity (Aadhaar), Payments (UPI), and Data Exchange (DigiLocker, Account Aggregator).
UPI is a real-time payment system developed by NPCI (National Payments Corporation of India).
Aadhaar is a 12-digit unique identification number issued by UIDAI (Unique Identification Authority of India).
DigiLocker provides a secure cloud-based platform for issuance and verification of documents.
India promotes DPI as a global public good, especially among Global South nations.
mainsPoints: Analyze how India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) has transformed governance, financial inclusion, and service delivery within the country.
Discuss the potential and challenges of replicating India's DPI model in other developing nations, considering diverse socio-economic contexts.
Evaluate the role of DPI in achieving Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and bridging the global digital divide.
Examine the critical issues of data privacy, cybersecurity, and ethical AI in the context of expanding DPI.
Suggest policy measures for international cooperation to foster open, inclusive, and interoperable digital ecosystems globally.
articles.imageDescription: Infographic showing the components of India Stack (Aadhaar, UPI, DigiLocker).
articles.trivia: UPI processed over 10 billion transactions in August 2023, making it one of the largest digital payment systems globally.

articles.headline: National Green Hydrogen Mission: India Accelerates Towards a Sustainable Energy Future
articles.explainer:
• India's National Green Hydrogen Mission is gaining momentum, with significant investments and policy support for green hydrogen production.
• The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, reducing reliance on fossil fuels.
• Focus areas include developing pilot projects, R&D, manufacturing of electrolyzers, and creating a robust regulatory framework.
• Challenges involve high production costs, establishing supply chains, and developing adequate infrastructure for storage and transport.
• Green hydrogen is crucial for decarbonizing hard-to-abate sectors like fertilizers, refining, and steel.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation, Environmental Impact Assessment
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
articles.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission launched in January 2023 with an outlay of Rs 19,744 crore.
Aim to make India a global hub for production, utilization, and export of Green Hydrogen.
Target to produce 5 MMT (million metric tonnes) of Green Hydrogen per annum by 2030.
Green Hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water using renewable energy.
It is crucial for decarbonizing heavy industries and achieving India's net-zero targets.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of the National Green Hydrogen Mission for India's energy security and climate change commitments.
Discuss the technological, economic, and infrastructural challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and adoption in India.
Evaluate the potential of green hydrogen to decarbonize various industrial sectors and its role in India's energy transition.
Examine the policy and regulatory framework required to support the growth of a robust green hydrogen ecosystem.
Suggest measures to reduce the cost of green hydrogen production and enhance its competitiveness with fossil fuels.
articles.imageDescription: Industrial plant producing green hydrogen with wind turbines in the background.
articles.trivia: India aims to achieve Net Zero emissions by 2070, with green hydrogen playing a vital role in this target.

articles.headline: India's Space Sector Reforms Propel ISRO and Private Players Towards New Frontiers
articles.explainer:
• Ongoing space sector reforms are transforming India's capabilities, fostering collaboration between ISRO and private industries.
• Policies like the Indian Space Policy 2023 aim to enable private sector participation across the entire value chain of space activities.
• Private companies are increasingly involved in satellite manufacturing, launch vehicle development, and ground segment operations.
• ISRO focuses on advanced research, interplanetary missions, and human spaceflight programs like Gaganyaan.
• The reforms are expected to boost India's share in the global space economy and promote innovation.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
keyTerms: Indian National Space Promotion and Authorization Center (IN-SPACe)
prelimsPoints: IN-SPACe is an independent nodal agency under the Department of Space for private sector participation.
NSIL is the commercial arm of ISRO, responsible for marketing space products and services.
Indian Space Policy 2023 aims to liberalize the space sector.
Gaganyaan mission aims to demonstrate human spaceflight capability by launching a crew of three members into orbit.
PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) and GSLV (Geosynchronous Satellite Launch Vehicle) are ISRO's operational launch vehicles.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of India's space sector reforms in boosting its capabilities and global competitiveness.
Discuss the opportunities and challenges for private players in collaborating with ISRO and contributing to the space economy.
Evaluate the potential of India's space program to drive technological innovation, economic growth, and national security.
Examine the role of agencies like IN-SPACe and NSIL in facilitating private sector engagement in space activities.
How can India ensure a sustainable and equitable growth of its space sector while addressing ethical and environmental concerns?
articles.imageDescription: A rocket launching from Sriharikota, symbolizing India's space ambitions.
articles.trivia: India's Mars Orbiter Mission (Mangalyaan) was the first Asian nation to reach Martian orbit and the first country in the world to do so in its first attempt.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Delivers Key Rulings on Social Justice and Constitutional Rights
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India continues its role as the guardian of the constitution, delivering significant judgments on social justice.
• Recent rulings address issues such as reservations, fundamental rights, and the protection of vulnerable sections of society.
• The court emphasizes the balance between individual liberties and collective welfare, upholding the spirit of the Constitution.
• These judgments often have far-reaching implications for government policies, legislative actions, and societal norms.
• Legal experts analyze the evolving jurisprudence on social justice, highlighting the court's activism and interpretation of constitutional principles.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court is the highest judicial court and the final court of appeal under the Constitution of India.
It acts as the guardian of the Constitution and the fundamental rights of citizens.
Article 32 grants the right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of fundamental rights.
Judicial review is the power of the Supreme Court to examine the constitutionality of legislative enactments and executive orders.
Landmark judgments like Kesavananda Bharati case established the Basic Structure Doctrine.
mainsPoints: Analyze the role of the Supreme Court as an arbiter of social justice in a diverse and democratic society like India.
Discuss how the Supreme Court's interpretation of fundamental rights has evolved over time, impacting various aspects of governance and society.
Critically evaluate the concept of 'judicial activism' in the context of the Supreme Court's rulings on social justice issues.
Examine the challenges faced by the judiciary in balancing individual rights with collective societal interests.
Suggest reforms to enhance the efficiency and accessibility of the justice delivery system while upholding its independence.
articles.imageDescription: The Supreme Court of India building, symbolizing justice and law.
articles.trivia: The Supreme Court of India commenced sitting on 28 January 1950, two days after India became a Sovereign Democratic Republic.

articles.headline: India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) Gains Momentum, Reshaping Global Trade Routes
articles.explainer:
• The ambitious India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) is progressing, with key stakeholders affirming commitment to its development.
• IMEC aims to connect India, the Middle East, and Europe via a network of sea and rail links, enhancing trade and economic cooperation.
• The corridor is seen as a strategic alternative to existing trade routes, reducing transit times and logistics costs.
• Geopolitical implications include strengthening partnerships among participating nations and fostering regional stability.
• Challenges involve significant infrastructure investment, coordination among diverse partners, and addressing potential security concerns.
gsSubTopics: Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: IMEC was announced during the G20 Summit in New Delhi in September 2023.
It involves a multimodal transport corridor comprising a railway and shipping line.
Key partners include India, USA, Saudi Arabia, UAE, France, Germany, Italy, and the European Union.
Aims to enhance trade, energy, and digital connectivity between Asia, the Arabian Gulf, and Europe.
It is seen as a counter-narrative to China's Belt and Road Initiative (BRI).
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic and economic significance of the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) for India and its partners.
Discuss the geopolitical implications of IMEC in reshaping global trade routes and fostering regional stability.
Evaluate the potential challenges in the implementation of IMEC, including financing, coordination, and security concerns.
Compare and contrast IMEC with other major connectivity initiatives like China's Belt and Road Initiative (BRI).
Suggest measures for effective collaboration among participating nations to ensure the timely and successful completion of IMEC.
articles.imageDescription: Map showing the proposed route of the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor.
articles.trivia: The Suez Canal, a vital shipping lane, currently handles approximately 12% of global trade.

articles.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) Expands Reach, Focuses on Universal Health Coverage
articles.explainer:
• The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) is making strides in creating a digital health ecosystem across India.
• The mission aims to provide every citizen with a unique Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA) number for integrated health records.
• Focus is on seamless interoperability of health data, empowering patients, and improving access to healthcare services.
• Challenges include digital literacy, data security concerns, and ensuring adoption by healthcare providers.
• Government emphasizes public-private partnerships and technological innovation to achieve universal health coverage.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: ABDM was launched nationwide on September 27, 2021, by Prime Minister Narendra Modi.
It aims to develop the backbone necessary to support the integrated digital health infrastructure of the country.
Key components include ABHA number, Healthcare Professionals Registry (HPR), Health Facility Registry (HFR).
Implemented by the National Health Authority (NHA) under the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare.
It is a voluntary opt-in program for citizens.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) in revolutionizing India's healthcare delivery system.
Discuss the challenges associated with implementing a comprehensive digital health ecosystem, including data privacy and digital literacy.
Evaluate the role of ABHA in empowering patients and improving access to healthcare services, particularly in remote areas.
Examine how ABDM can facilitate universal health coverage and strengthen the public health infrastructure.
Suggest measures to ensure the security, interoperability, and widespread adoption of digital health solutions across all segments of society.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic illustrating the interconnected digital health ecosystem of ABDM.
articles.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB PM-JAY) is the world's largest government-funded health assurance scheme.

articles.headline: Agricultural Reforms and Farmer Welfare: Government Continues Focus on Income Enhancement and Market Access
articles.explainer:
• The government reiterates its commitment to farmer welfare, focusing on income enhancement and modernization of the agricultural sector.
• Discussions center on improving Minimum Support Price (MSP) mechanisms, diversifying crops, and promoting sustainable farming practices.
• Initiatives aim to strengthen agricultural infrastructure, including storage, cold chains, and e-NAM platforms for better market access.
• Challenges persist in addressing issues like climate change impact, fragmented landholdings, and farmer indebtedness.
• Policy measures are being explored to encourage private investment in agriculture and agri-tech startups.
gsSubTopics: Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System— objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: MSP is announced by the Government of India for 22 mandated crops and Fair and Remunerative Price (FRP) for sugarcane.
Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP) recommends MSPs.
e-NAM is an online trading platform for agricultural commodities.
PMFBY provides insurance coverage and financial support to farmers in the event of crop failure.
FPOs are groups of farmers who come together to undertake farming and related activities collectively.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the government's agricultural reforms aimed at doubling farmers' income and enhancing their welfare.
Discuss the challenges in ensuring remunerative prices for farmers, particularly concerning the Minimum Support Price (MSP) mechanism.
Evaluate the role of technology, such as e-NAM and agri-tech startups, in modernizing Indian agriculture and improving market access.
Examine the impact of climate change on agricultural productivity and the strategies adopted for sustainable farming practices.
Suggest comprehensive policy measures to address issues of farmer indebtedness, fragmentation of landholdings, and rural distress.
articles.imageDescription: A farmer working in a lush green field, symbolizing agricultural prosperity.
articles.trivia: India is the world's largest producer of milk, pulses, and jute, and the second-largest producer of rice, wheat, sugarcane, groundnut, fruits, and vegetables.

articles.headline: National Education Policy (NEP) 2020: Focus on Skill Development and Multilingualism for Future-Ready Youth
articles.explainer:
• Implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 continues to be a priority, emphasizing holistic development.
• The policy promotes skill development, vocational education, and multidisciplinary learning to enhance employability.
• Special focus is given to multilingualism and the promotion of regional languages, from foundational stages to higher education.
• Challenges include infrastructural gaps, faculty training, and ensuring equitable access to quality education across all regions.
• Government aims to create a flexible, learner-centric education system that prepares youth for the 21st-century workforce.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaces the National Policy on Education of 1986.
It proposes a new 5+3+3+4 curricular structure instead of 10+2.
Emphasizes mother tongue/local language as medium of instruction till at least Grade 5.
Aims for universalization of education from pre-school to secondary level with 100% Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) by 2030.
Recommends setting up of National Research Foundation (NRF) to foster a strong research culture.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of the National Education Policy 2020 in addressing the challenges of India's education system.
Discuss how NEP 2020's emphasis on skill development and vocational education can enhance the employability of Indian youth.
Evaluate the policy's approach to multilingualism and the promotion of regional languages in fostering cultural identity and cognitive development.
Examine the implementation challenges of NEP 2020, including funding, teacher training, and digital infrastructure.
Suggest strategies to ensure equitable access to quality education and bridge the learning gap across different socio-economic strata under NEP 2020.
articles.imageDescription: Students engaged in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing holistic education.
articles.trivia: India has one of the largest higher education systems in the world, with over 1,000 universities.

articles.headline: Border Infrastructure Development: Enhancing Connectivity and Security Along India's Frontiers
articles.explainer:
• India continues to prioritize robust border infrastructure development to enhance national security and economic connectivity.
• Projects include construction of roads, bridges, tunnels, and advanced surveillance systems in strategic border areas.
• The development aims to facilitate quicker troop movement, improve logistics for border forces, and integrate remote regions.
• Geopolitical considerations and ongoing border disputes influence the pace and nature of infrastructure projects.
• Challenges involve harsh terrain, extreme weather conditions, and maintaining ecological balance in sensitive areas.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
prelimsPoints: Border Roads Organisation (BRO) constructs and maintains road networks in border areas.
BRO works under the Ministry of Defence.
India shares land borders with seven countries: Pakistan, Afghanistan (disputed), China, Nepal, Bhutan, Myanmar, Bangladesh.
Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) aims for comprehensive and integrated border security.
The 'Vibrant Villages Programme' aims to develop border villages into vibrant economic centers.
mainsPoints: Analyze the critical role of border infrastructure development in strengthening India's national security and strategic capabilities.
Discuss the challenges faced in constructing and maintaining infrastructure in difficult terrains along India's borders.
Evaluate the impact of improved border connectivity on the socio-economic development of remote border regions and their integration with the mainstream.
Examine how border infrastructure development influences India's geopolitical standing and its relations with neighboring countries.
Suggest measures to balance strategic infrastructure development with environmental conservation and the concerns of local communities in border areas.
articles.imageDescription: A newly constructed bridge in a mountainous border region, facilitating military movement.
articles.trivia: The Atal Tunnel, connecting Manali to Lahaul-Spiti valley, is the world's longest highway single-tube tunnel above 10,000 feet.

articles.headline: Conservation of Biodiversity: Project Tiger and Project Elephant Showcase India's Commitment to Wildlife Protection
articles.explainer:
• India continues its robust efforts in wildlife conservation, exemplified by the success of Project Tiger and Project Elephant.
• These flagship programs have significantly contributed to the recovery of tiger and elephant populations and their habitats.
• Focus extends to addressing human-wildlife conflict, combating poaching, and protecting critical wildlife corridors.
• India's conservation model emphasizes community participation and sustainable management of natural resources.
• New initiatives are being explored to protect other endangered species and enhance biodiversity conservation across various ecosystems.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
articles.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: Project Tiger launched in 1973; Project Elephant launched in 1992.
India is home to over 70% of the world's tiger population and about 60% of Asian elephants.
National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA) is a statutory body under MoEF&CC, constituted under the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.
The Wild Life (Protection) Act, 1972, provides for the protection of wild animals, birds, and plants.
India has 54 Tiger Reserves and 33 Elephant Reserves (as of 2023 data).
mainsPoints: Analyze the success story of Project Tiger and Project Elephant in India and their contribution to global biodiversity conservation efforts.
Discuss the challenges faced in wildlife conservation, including human-wildlife conflict, poaching, and habitat fragmentation.
Evaluate the role of community participation and eco-tourism in promoting sustainable wildlife management.
Examine the legal and institutional framework for wildlife protection in India and suggest areas for improvement.
Suggest innovative strategies to protect endangered species and critical wildlife corridors in the face of climate change and developmental pressures.
articles.imageDescription: A majestic tiger in its natural habitat, symbolizing wildlife protection.
articles.trivia: The Bengal Tiger is India's national animal.

articles.headline: Strengthening India's Cooperative Movement for Inclusive Rural Development and Economic Empowerment
articles.explainer:
• The cooperative sector in India is being reinvigorated with new policies aimed at strengthening its role in rural development.
• Focus is on promoting primary agricultural credit societies (PACS), dairy, fisheries, and other cooperative enterprises.
• Initiatives aim to enhance financial inclusion, provide market linkages, and empower farmers and marginalized communities.
• Challenges include governance issues, lack of professional management, and competition from private entities.
• Government emphasizes digitization and diversification of cooperative activities to unlock their full potential.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: The 97th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2011, gave constitutional status and protection to cooperative societies.
Article 19(1)(c) includes the right to form cooperative societies.
Article 43B was added as a DPSP, promoting voluntary formation, autonomous functioning, democratic control, and professional management of cooperative societies.
A separate Ministry of Cooperation was formed in July 2021.
NABARD provides financial support to cooperative banks and PACS.
mainsPoints: Analyze the historical evolution and significance of the cooperative movement in India's rural development and economic empowerment.
Discuss the challenges faced by cooperative societies, including governance issues, financial constraints, and lack of professionalism.
Evaluate the government's initiatives to strengthen the cooperative sector and unlock its potential for inclusive growth.
Examine how cooperatives can play a crucial role in financial inclusion, food security, and market access for farmers.
Suggest reforms to enhance the efficiency, transparency, and democratic functioning of cooperative societies in India.
articles.imageDescription: Farmers collaborating in a field, symbolizing cooperative spirit.
articles.trivia: Amul, a dairy cooperative movement, is India's largest food product organization and a global leader in dairy.

articles.headline: Indian Judiciary Emphasizes Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) for Efficient Justice Delivery
articles.explainer:
• The Indian judiciary is increasingly promoting Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) mechanisms to reduce case backlogs and provide speedy justice.
• ADR methods like mediation, conciliation, arbitration, and Lok Adalats are being encouraged for various civil and commercial disputes.
• The aim is to make justice accessible, affordable, and less time-consuming for citizens.
• Challenges include public awareness, training of ADR professionals, and ensuring enforceability of settlements.
• Policy reforms are underway to strengthen the legal framework for ADR and integrate it more effectively into the judicial system.
gsSubTopics: Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary Ministries and Departments of the Government; pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity.
Mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: ADR includes arbitration, conciliation, mediation, negotiation, and Lok Adalat.
Lok Adalats are statutory bodies constituted under the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987.
Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, governs arbitration and conciliation in India.
The concept of Nyaya Panchayats in rural areas is a form of local dispute resolution.
ADR aims to provide speedy and cost-effective justice.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) mechanisms in enhancing the efficiency and accessibility of India's justice delivery system.
Discuss the various forms of ADR prevalent in India and their specific advantages and limitations.
Evaluate the challenges in promoting widespread adoption of ADR, including lack of awareness and training of professionals.
Examine the role of Lok Adalats in providing speedy and affordable justice to the common person.
Suggest policy and institutional reforms to strengthen the legal framework and infrastructure for ADR in India.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel and scales of justice, symbolizing the legal system and alternative dispute resolution.
articles.trivia: The first Lok Adalat was held in Gujarat in 1982.

articles.headline: India-Sri Lanka Economic Cooperation Deepens Amidst Regional Stability Initiatives
articles.explainer:
• India and Sri Lanka are enhancing economic cooperation, focusing on trade, investment, and infrastructure projects.
• Discussions include connectivity projects, energy collaboration, and support for Sri Lanka's economic recovery.
• India reaffirms its 'Neighbourhood First' policy, providing assistance and fostering bilateral ties.
• Geopolitical significance of the Indian Ocean Region drives strategic engagement between the two nations.
• Challenges include addressing concerns of the fishing communities and ensuring equitable benefits from joint projects.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy prioritizes relations with immediate neighbors.
India has provided significant financial assistance to Sri Lanka for its economic recovery.
Key areas of cooperation include connectivity, energy, tourism, and defence.
The Palk Strait separates India and Sri Lanka.
Trincomalee Oil Tank Farm is a joint development project between India and Sri Lanka.
mainsPoints: Analyze the various facets of India-Sri Lanka economic cooperation and its significance for regional stability in the Indian Ocean Region.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in bilateral relations, including issues related to fishing rights and strategic competition.
Evaluate India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy in the context of its engagement with Sri Lanka and its role in fostering regional development.
Examine the potential of connectivity projects and energy collaboration to deepen bilateral ties and promote mutual prosperity.
Suggest measures to enhance trust and cooperation between India and Sri Lanka, addressing long-standing issues and exploring new avenues of partnership.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and Sri Lanka waving together, symbolizing friendship.
articles.trivia: The Ram Setu (Adam's Bridge) is a chain of natural limestone shoals between Pamban Island (India) and Mannar Island (Sri Lanka).

articles.headline: Promoting Climate-Resilient Agriculture to Ensure Food Security Amidst Changing Weather Patterns
articles.explainer:
• India is intensifying efforts to promote climate-resilient agriculture to safeguard food security against extreme weather events.
• Strategies include developing drought-resistant crops, water-efficient irrigation techniques, and climate-smart farming practices.
• Focus is on research and development, capacity building for farmers, and integrating traditional knowledge with modern science.
• Challenges involve scaling up these practices, financial constraints for small and marginal farmers, and unpredictable climate patterns.
• Government policies aim to build a sustainable and adaptive agricultural sector capable of withstanding climate shocks.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
prelimsPoints: Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) is an autonomous organization under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare.
National Innovations in Climate Resilient Agriculture (NICRA) is an ICAR project.
Climate-smart agriculture includes practices like conservation tillage, crop rotation, integrated nutrient management.
Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY) focuses on 'per drop more crop'.
India is vulnerable to climate change impacts like droughts, floods, and heatwaves.
mainsPoints: Analyze the threats posed by climate change to India's food security and agricultural sustainability.
Discuss the various strategies and technologies being adopted to promote climate-resilient agriculture in India.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government schemes and research initiatives in empowering farmers to adapt to changing weather patterns.
Examine the challenges in scaling up climate-resilient practices, especially for small and marginal farmers, and ensuring financial viability.
Suggest comprehensive policy interventions to build a robust and adaptive agricultural sector capable of ensuring long-term food security.
articles.imageDescription: Farmers inspecting a healthy crop field under an advanced irrigation system.
articles.trivia: India is one of the world's top producers of several agricultural commodities, but also highly vulnerable to climate change impacts on agriculture.

articles.headline: Urban Governance Reforms: Challenges and Opportunities in India's Smart Cities Mission
articles.explainer:
• India's Smart Cities Mission continues to drive urban development, focusing on sustainable and citizen-friendly urban spaces.
• Reforms aim to improve urban governance, infrastructure, and livability through technology and participatory planning.
• Projects include smart mobility, waste management, digital services, and urban green spaces.
• Challenges persist in funding, inter-agency coordination, citizen engagement, and addressing rapid urbanization.
• Emphasis is on making cities more resilient, inclusive, and environmentally sustainable for future generations.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Development processes and the development industry—the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.
articles.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: Smart Cities Mission launched in June 2015.
100 cities selected to be developed as Smart Cities.
Aims to promote cities that provide core infrastructure and give a decent quality of life to its citizens.
Uses Area Based Development (ABD) and Pan-City Solutions approaches.
Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs is the nodal ministry.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and achievements of India's Smart Cities Mission in transforming urban landscapes and improving livability.
Discuss the challenges faced in urban governance, including funding, capacity building, and citizen participation, within the context of the mission.
Evaluate the role of technology and data-driven solutions in making cities more efficient, sustainable, and inclusive.
Examine how the Smart Cities Mission addresses issues of rapid urbanization, infrastructure deficits, and environmental sustainability.
Suggest reforms and policy measures to overcome implementation hurdles and ensure the long-term success of urban development initiatives in India.
articles.imageDescription: A modern city skyline with green spaces, representing a smart and sustainable urban environment.
articles.trivia: India is projected to have 600 million people living in urban areas by 2030.

articles.headline: Boosting Indigenous Defence Manufacturing under 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' Initiative
articles.explainer:
• India is accelerating efforts to boost indigenous defence manufacturing, reducing reliance on foreign imports.
• The 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiative in defence aims to foster self-reliance through domestic production, R&D, and technology transfer.
• Policies include negative import lists, increased FDI in defence, and incentives for private sector participation.
• Focus areas are developing advanced weaponry, aerospace technologies, and critical defence components.
• Challenges involve technological gaps, ensuring quality standards, and building a robust defence industrial ecosystem.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: Atmanirbhar Bharat Abhiyan was launched in May 2020.
Defence Acquisition Procedure (DAP) 2020 prioritizes 'Buy Indian' and 'Make Indian' categories.
Negative import lists for defence items aim to promote domestic manufacturing.
DRDO is the R&D wing of the Ministry of Defence.
Two Defence Industrial Corridors are being established in Uttar Pradesh and Tamil Nadu.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic imperative and economic benefits of boosting indigenous defence manufacturing under the 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiative.
Discuss the key policy measures adopted by the government to promote self-reliance in the defence sector.
Evaluate the challenges faced in achieving complete indigenization of defence production, including technological gaps and quality control.
Examine the role of DRDO and private sector participation in strengthening India's defence industrial ecosystem.
Suggest strategies to enhance defence exports and integrate Indian defence manufacturing into global supply chains.
articles.imageDescription: An indigenously developed defence aircraft flying, symbolizing self-reliance.
articles.trivia: India is one of the world's largest arms importers, but aims to become a net exporter of defence equipment.

highlights: India commemorates Mahatma Gandhi's 155th Birth Anniversary, reaffirming principles of truth, non-violence, and Sarvodaya.
Swachh Bharat Mission marks a decade of progress in sanitation, with focus on sustainable waste management in Phase II.
Women's Reservation Act, 2023, implementation timeline and delimitation process become key discussion points.
RBI maintains status quo on repo rate, emphasizing inflation management amidst global economic uncertainties.
India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) model, including Aadhaar and UPI, gains global recognition for inclusive growth.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the International Day of Non-Violence, consider the following statements:
1. It is observed annually on October 2nd to commemorate the birth anniversary of Mahatma Gandhi.
2. The United Nations General Assembly declared this day in 2007.
3. The day primarily aims to promote peace and non-violence through education and public awareness.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. October 2nd is Mahatma Gandhi's birth anniversary and is observed as the International Day of Non-Violence. The UN General Assembly declared this day in 2007 (Resolution A/RES/61/271) to disseminate the message of non-violence, including through education and public awareness.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following bodies is responsible for implementing the Swachh Bharat Mission-Grameen (SBM-G)?
options: (a) Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
(b) Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Swachh Bharat Mission has two sub-missions: SBM-Urban, implemented by the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs, and SBM-Grameen (SBM-G), implemented by the Ministry of Jal Shakti (Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation).

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI):
1. Aadhaar provides the identity layer of India's DPI.
2. UPI is a real-time payment system developed by the Reserve Bank of India.
3. DigiLocker facilitates secure cloud-based storage and verification of documents.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: Aadhaar is the identity layer. Statement 3 is correct: DigiLocker is for document storage and verification. Statement 2 is incorrect: UPI (Unified Payments Interface) is developed by the National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI), not the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), although RBI regulates it.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Women's Reservation Act, 2023, consider the following statements:
1. It reserves 33% of seats for women in the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies.
2. The reservation will come into effect immediately upon the next general elections.
3. The Act mandates that the reserved seats will be rotated after each delimitation exercise.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. Statement 2 is incorrect; the reservation will come into effect after the next census and subsequent delimitation exercise, not immediately. Statement 3 is correct, as per the Act's provisions for rotational reservation.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of India's National Green Hydrogen Mission?
options:
(a) Developing a robust regulatory framework for green hydrogen.
(b) Promoting research and development in electrolyzer manufacturing.
(c) Providing subsidies for the production of 'grey hydrogen'.
(d) Establishing pilot projects for green hydrogen production and utilization.
prelimsMocks.answer: (c) Providing subsidies for the production of 'grey hydrogen'.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The National Green Hydrogen Mission specifically focuses on 'green hydrogen' (produced using renewable energy) and aims to reduce reliance on fossil fuels. 'Grey hydrogen' is produced from fossil fuels, emitting carbon, and would not be subsidized under this mission. The other options are key components of the mission.

mainsMocks.question: Gandhian ideals, though rooted in the early 20th century, hold profound relevance for addressing contemporary global challenges. Discuss this statement in the context of peace, sustainability, and inclusive development.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS1: Modern Indian history; GS2: India and its neighborhood- relations, Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections; GS3: Environmental pollution and degradation.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce Gandhi's core ideals (truth, non-violence, Sarvodaya, trusteeship, Swadeshi). Connect each ideal to a contemporary global challenge: non-violence to conflict resolution and terrorism; Sarvodaya to inclusive development and inequality; trusteeship and Swadeshi to sustainable consumption, environmental conservation, and local economies. Provide examples of how India and the world apply/could apply these ideals. Conclude on the enduring legacy and necessity of Gandhian thought.

mainsMocks.question: India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) has been hailed as a model for inclusive growth. Critically examine the potential and challenges of replicating the Indian DPI model in other developing countries, particularly in the Global South.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors; GS3: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life, Cyber security.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce India's DPI (Aadhaar, UPI, DigiLocker) and its success in financial inclusion and governance. Discuss its potential for other developing countries (cost-effective, scalable, interoperable). Highlight challenges: data privacy and security concerns, digital literacy gaps, regulatory frameworks, infrastructure availability, geopolitical considerations, and local socio-economic contexts. Conclude with recommendations for tailored approaches and international cooperation.

mainsMocks.question: The Women's Reservation Act, 2023, marks a significant step towards gender equality in political representation. However, its implementation faces several logistical and constitutional hurdles. Analyze these challenges and suggest measures to ensure its effective and timely operationalization.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure; Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the Women's Reservation Act, 2023 (Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam) and its objective. Explain the constitutional requirement of a fresh census and delimitation for its implementation. Discuss logistical challenges (timeframe for census, delimitation process, rotational reservation implications). Address constitutional debates and political will. Suggest measures like expedited census and delimitation, transparent rotation mechanisms, public awareness campaigns, and capacity building for women representatives.

quickBytes.text: Mahatma Gandhi's philosophy of 'Sarvodaya' means 'universal upliftment' or 'progress of all'.
The 106th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2023, is also known as the Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam.
India aims to achieve Net Zero emissions by the year 2070.
The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) of RBI has six members.
Project Tiger was launched in India in 1973 to conserve the Bengal Tiger.
The National Green Hydrogen Mission aims for 5 MMT production by 2030.
The Indian Space Policy 2023 aims to liberalize India's space sector for private participation.
The 'Neighbourhood First' policy is a key tenet of India's foreign policy.
Lok Adalats are statutory bodies constituted under the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987.
The new 5+3+3+4 curricular structure is proposed under the National Education Policy 2020.

GS1.headline: Mahatma Gandhi's 155th Birth Anniversary: Reaffirming Principles of Truth, Non-Violence, and Sarvodaya in Modern India
GS1.explainer:
• India commemorates the 155th birth anniversary of Mahatma Gandhi, the Father of the Nation, with various events across the country.
• The day is globally observed as the International Day of Non-Violence, emphasizing Gandhi's philosophy of peace and satyagraha.
• Discussions focus on the contemporary relevance of Gandhian ideals in addressing global challenges like conflict, inequality, and environmental degradation.
• Government initiatives like Swachh Bharat Abhiyan and promotion of Khadi are often linked to Gandhi's vision of rural upliftment and self-reliance.
• Leaders pay homage at Raj Ghat, highlighting Gandhi's enduring legacy in India's freedom struggle and nation-building.
gsSubTopics: Modern Indian history from about the middle of the eighteenth century until the present- significant events, personalities, issues
The Freedom Struggle - its various stages and important contributors/contributions from different parts of the country
prelimsPoints: Mahatma Gandhi's birth date: October 2, 1869, Porbandar, Gujarat.
International Day of Non-Violence declared by UN General Assembly in 2007.
Key Gandhian principles: Truth (Satya), Non-violence (Ahimsa), Satyagraha, Sarvodaya (welfare of all), Swadeshi.
Important movements led by Gandhi: Champaran, Kheda, Non-Cooperation, Civil Disobedience, Quit India.
Raj Ghat in Delhi is the memorial to Mahatma Gandhi.
mainsPoints: Discuss the enduring relevance of Gandhian philosophy in contemporary India's socio-economic and political landscape.
Analyze how Gandhi's principles of non-violence and truth can be applied to resolve modern-day conflicts and promote global peace.
Evaluate the success and challenges of government initiatives (e.g., Swachh Bharat, Khadi promotion) in embodying Gandhian ideals.
Examine Gandhi's concept of 'Sarvodaya' and its implications for inclusive development and social justice.
Critically assess the role of Gandhi's philosophy in shaping India's foreign policy, particularly its emphasis on peace and multilateralism.
GS1.imageDescription: Mahatma Gandhi's portrait with a spinning wheel, symbolizing his ideals of self-reliance and peace.
GS1.trivia: Mahatma Gandhi was nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize five times between 1937 and 1948 but never received it.

GS2.headline: Swachh Bharat Mission: A Decade of Progress and the Road Ahead for Sustainable Sanitation
GS2.explainer:
• Launched on Gandhi Jayanti in 2014, the Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM) completes a decade, marking significant strides in sanitation coverage.
• The mission achieved Open Defecation Free (ODF) status in rural areas, leading to improved public health indicators.
• Phase II of SBM (SBM-Grameen Phase II and SBM-Urban 2.0) focuses on sustaining ODF status and holistic solid and liquid waste management.
• Challenges remain in behavioral change, infrastructure maintenance, and transitioning towards a circular economy for waste.
• Government emphasizes citizen participation (Jan Andolan) and technological solutions for effective waste management.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: SBM launched on Oct 2, 2014.
Two sub-missions: SBM-Urban (Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs) and SBM-Grameen (Ministry of Jal Shakti).
SBM Phase I focused on achieving ODF status.
SBM Phase II (2020-21 to 2024-25) focuses on ODF Plus, ODF Plus Plus, and SLWM.
ODF Plus aims for visual cleanliness and ODF Plus Plus for safe containment and treatment of fecal sludge.
mainsPoints: Analyze the impact of Swachh Bharat Mission on public health, gender equality, and environmental sustainability in India.
Discuss the challenges in achieving and sustaining 'ODF Plus' status across all regions, particularly in urban areas.
Evaluate the role of behavioral change communication and community participation in the success of SBM.
Suggest innovative approaches for solid and liquid waste management to transition towards a circular economy model.
Examine how SBM aligns with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly SDG 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation).
GS2.imageDescription: A graphic depicting the evolution of Swachh Bharat Mission from ODF to ODF Plus.
GS2.trivia: Swachh Bharat Abhiyan is the largest sanitation initiative ever undertaken by any country.

GS2.headline: Global Call for Peace: UN Observes International Day of Non-Violence on Gandhi Jayanti
GS2.explainer:
• The United Nations marks October 2nd as the International Day of Non-Violence, reinforcing Mahatma Gandhi's message of peace.
• The day serves as a reminder to resolve conflicts through peaceful means, dialogue, and mutual understanding.
• UN Secretary-General issues a statement, urging nations to uphold principles of non-violence in an increasingly complex global landscape.
• India plays a pivotal role in advocating for non-violence in international forums, drawing from its Gandhian heritage.
• Educational institutions and civil society organizations worldwide organize events to promote non-violence and tolerance.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: UN General Assembly Resolution A/RES/61/271 adopted on 15 June 2007, establishing October 2 as International Day of Non-Violence.
The resolution reaffirms 'the universal relevance of the principle of non-violence'.
The day aims to 'disseminate the message of non-violence, including through education and public awareness'.
Mahatma Gandhi's philosophy of non-violence is central to the observance.
UN promotes the culture of peace through various initiatives.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the International Day of Non-Violence in promoting global peace and security in the 21st century.
Analyze India's contribution to global peace initiatives and its diplomatic efforts rooted in Gandhian principles.
Examine the challenges to non-violence in a world grappling with terrorism, geopolitical tensions, and social unrest.
Evaluate the role of international organizations like the UN in fostering a culture of peace and tolerance.
How can education and public awareness campaigns effectively propagate the message of non-violence among youth globally?
GS2.imageDescription: UN logo alongside a dove of peace, symbolizing international cooperation for non-violence.
GS2.trivia: The phrase 'Ahimsa Paramo Dharma' (non-violence is the highest moral virtue) is a central tenet of Jainism, influencing Gandhi.

GS2.headline: Women's Reservation Act, 2023: Delimitation Process and Implementation Timeline in Focus for 2024 Elections
GS2.explainer:
• Following the passage of the Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam (Women's Reservation Act) in 2023, discussions intensify on its implementation.
• The Act mandates 33% reservation for women in Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies, but requires a fresh census and delimitation.
• Political parties and civil society groups debate the timeline for delimitation and its potential impact on electoral constituencies.
• Concerns are raised regarding the delay in implementation and the need for clarity on rotational reservation.
• The Election Commission of India and the Delimitation Commission are expected to play crucial roles in this process.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
prelimsPoints: The 128th Constitutional Amendment Bill, 2023, became the Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam (106th Constitutional Amendment Act).
It reserves one-third of seats for women in Lok Sabha, State Assemblies, and Delhi Assembly.
The reservation will come into effect after the next census and subsequent delimitation exercise.
The reservation will be for a period of 15 years and can be extended by Parliament.
Seats will be reserved on a rotational basis after each delimitation exercise.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the significance and potential impact of the Women's Reservation Act on gender equality and political representation in India.
Discuss the constitutional and logistical challenges involved in the delimitation process and its implications for the Act's implementation.
Evaluate the arguments for and against the necessity of a fresh census and delimitation for the Act's operationalization.
Examine how the rotational reservation of seats for women might affect political stability and constituency development.
Suggest measures to ensure the effective and timely implementation of the Women's Reservation Act to empower women in governance.
GS2.imageDescription: Symbolic image of women participating in parliamentary proceedings.
GS2.trivia: The concept of women's reservation was first introduced in India in local self-governing bodies through the 73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendment Acts.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Delivers Key Rulings on Social Justice and Constitutional Rights
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India continues its role as the guardian of the constitution, delivering significant judgments on social justice.
• Recent rulings address issues such as reservations, fundamental rights, and the protection of vulnerable sections of society.
• The court emphasizes the balance between individual liberties and collective welfare, upholding the spirit of the Constitution.
• These judgments often have far-reaching implications for government policies, legislative actions, and societal norms.
• Legal experts analyze the evolving jurisprudence on social justice, highlighting the court's activism and interpretation of constitutional principles.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court is the highest judicial court and the final court of appeal under the Constitution of India.
It acts as the guardian of the Constitution and the fundamental rights of citizens.
Article 32 grants the right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of fundamental rights.
Judicial review is the power of the Supreme Court to examine the constitutionality of legislative enactments and executive orders.
Landmark judgments like Kesavananda Bharati case established the Basic Structure Doctrine.
mainsPoints: Analyze the role of the Supreme Court as an arbiter of social justice in a diverse and democratic society like India.
Discuss how the Supreme Court's interpretation of fundamental rights has evolved over time, impacting various aspects of governance and society.
Critically evaluate the concept of 'judicial activism' in the context of the Supreme Court's rulings on social justice issues.
Examine the challenges faced by the judiciary in balancing individual rights with collective societal interests.
Suggest reforms to enhance the efficiency and accessibility of the justice delivery system while upholding its independence.
GS2.imageDescription: The Supreme Court of India building, symbolizing justice and law.
GS2.trivia: The Supreme Court of India commenced sitting on 28 January 1950, two days after India became a Sovereign Democratic Republic.

GS2.headline: India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) Gains Momentum, Reshaping Global Trade Routes
GS2.explainer:
• The ambitious India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) is progressing, with key stakeholders affirming commitment to its development.
• IMEC aims to connect India, the Middle East, and Europe via a network of sea and rail links, enhancing trade and economic cooperation.
• The corridor is seen as a strategic alternative to existing trade routes, reducing transit times and logistics costs.
• Geopolitical implications include strengthening partnerships among participating nations and fostering regional stability.
• Challenges involve significant infrastructure investment, coordination among diverse partners, and addressing potential security concerns.
gsSubTopics: Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: IMEC was announced during the G20 Summit in New Delhi in September 2023.
It involves a multimodal transport corridor comprising a railway and shipping line.
Key partners include India, USA, Saudi Arabia, UAE, France, Germany, Italy, and the European Union.
Aims to enhance trade, energy, and digital connectivity between Asia, the Arabian Gulf, and Europe.
It is seen as a counter-narrative to China's Belt and Road Initiative (BRI).
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic and economic significance of the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) for India and its partners.
Discuss the geopolitical implications of IMEC in reshaping global trade routes and fostering regional stability.
Evaluate the potential challenges in the implementation of IMEC, including financing, coordination, and security concerns.
Compare and contrast IMEC with other major connectivity initiatives like China's Belt and Road Initiative (BRI).
Suggest measures for effective collaboration among participating nations to ensure the timely and successful completion of IMEC.
GS2.imageDescription: Map showing the proposed route of the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor.
GS2.trivia: The Suez Canal, a vital shipping lane, currently handles approximately 12% of global trade.

GS2.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) Expands Reach, Focuses on Universal Health Coverage
GS2.explainer:
• The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) is making strides in creating a digital health ecosystem across India.
• The mission aims to provide every citizen with a unique Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA) number for integrated health records.
• Focus is on seamless interoperability of health data, empowering patients, and improving access to healthcare services.
• Challenges include digital literacy, data security concerns, and ensuring adoption by healthcare providers.
• Government emphasizes public-private partnerships and technological innovation to achieve universal health coverage.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: ABDM was launched nationwide on September 27, 2021, by Prime Minister Narendra Modi.
It aims to develop the backbone necessary to support the integrated digital health infrastructure of the country.
Key components include ABHA number, Healthcare Professionals Registry (HPR), Health Facility Registry (HFR).
Implemented by the National Health Authority (NHA) under the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare.
It is a voluntary opt-in program for citizens.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) in revolutionizing India's healthcare delivery system.
Discuss the challenges associated with implementing a comprehensive digital health ecosystem, including data privacy and digital literacy.
Evaluate the role of ABHA in empowering patients and improving access to healthcare services, particularly in remote areas.
Examine how ABDM can facilitate universal health coverage and strengthen the public health infrastructure.
Suggest measures to ensure the security, interoperability, and widespread adoption of digital health solutions across all segments of society.
GS2.imageDescription: A graphic illustrating the interconnected digital health ecosystem of ABDM.
GS2.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB PM-JAY) is the world's largest government-funded health assurance scheme.

GS2.headline: National Education Policy (NEP) 2020: Focus on Skill Development and Multilingualism for Future-Ready Youth
GS2.explainer:
• Implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 continues to be a priority, emphasizing holistic development.
• The policy promotes skill development, vocational education, and multidisciplinary learning to enhance employability.
• Special focus is given to multilingualism and the promotion of regional languages, from foundational stages to higher education.
• Challenges include infrastructural gaps, faculty training, and ensuring equitable access to quality education across all regions.
• Government aims to create a flexible, learner-centric education system that prepares youth for the 21st-century workforce.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaces the National Policy on Education of 1986.
It proposes a new 5+3+3+4 curricular structure instead of 10+2.
Emphasizes mother tongue/local language as medium of instruction till at least Grade 5.
Aims for universalization of education from pre-school to secondary level with 100% Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) by 2030.
Recommends setting up of National Research Foundation (NRF) to foster a strong research culture.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of the National Education Policy 2020 in addressing the challenges of India's education system.
Discuss how NEP 2020's emphasis on skill development and vocational education can enhance the employability of Indian youth.
Evaluate the policy's approach to multilingualism and the promotion of regional languages in fostering cultural identity and cognitive development.
Examine the implementation challenges of NEP 2020, including funding, teacher training, and digital infrastructure.
Suggest strategies to ensure equitable access to quality education and bridge the learning gap across different socio-economic strata under NEP 2020.
GS2.imageDescription: Students engaged in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing holistic education.
GS2.trivia: India has one of the largest higher education systems in the world, with over 1,000 universities.

GS2.headline: Strengthening India's Cooperative Movement for Inclusive Rural Development and Economic Empowerment
GS2.explainer:
• The cooperative sector in India is being reinvigorated with new policies aimed at strengthening its role in rural development.
• Focus is on promoting primary agricultural credit societies (PACS), dairy, fisheries, and other cooperative enterprises.
• Initiatives aim to enhance financial inclusion, provide market linkages, and empower farmers and marginalized communities.
• Challenges include governance issues, lack of professional management, and competition from private entities.
• Government emphasizes digitization and diversification of cooperative activities to unlock their full potential.
